one_line_takeaway: SEAL is a black-box jailbreak that stacks multiple ciphers and uses a gradient-bandit adaptive selector to overwhelm reasoning models' decryption-based safety, hitting up to 100% ASR on DeepSeek-R1 and Gemini and 80.8% on o4-mini (27.2 points over prior SOTA), while empirically showing stronger reasoning increases vulnerability to complex encrypted attacks.
key_finding: SEAL-adaptive substantially outperforms all baselines across every evaluated LRM. On the curated 125-prompt high-risk subset it reaches ASRs of 80.8% (o4-mini), 84.8% (o1-mini), 85.6% (Claude 3.7 Sonnet), 84.0% (Claude 3.5 Sonnet), 79.2% (Gemini 2.0 Flash M), and 100% on both Gemini 2.0 Flash (H) and DeepSeek-R1. On o4-mini this is a 27.2-point margin over the strongest baseline (AutoDAN, 53.6%). Ablations show a non-monotonic pattern: ASR is near-zero at cipher length 1, jumps ~50-60 points at length 2, and beyond length 4 the RANDOM strategy collapses (prompts become too complex to decode) while the ADAPTIVE strategy keeps succeeding, peaking at length 6 -- evidence for a complexity 'sweet spot'. Recovery rate steadily declines with cipher length (Sonnet 3.7 recovers only ~half at length 6). Reasoning mode raised the ASR peak (>65%, shifted from length 2 to 3) versus non-reasoning mode, supporting the claim that reasoning increases vulnerability to complex encrypted attacks.
core_contribution: The paper proposes SEAL (Stacked Encryption for Adaptive Language reasoning model jailbreak), a black-box jailbreak attack tailored to Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) such as DeepSeek-R1, OpenAI o1/o4-mini, Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet, and Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking. The central idea is to bury the harmful intent under multiple stacked layers of ciphers whose combined complexity outstrips the model's decryption/reasoning ability enough to slip past safety alignment, yet remains recoverable enough that the model still produces useful harmful output. SEAL's own novelty over prior cipher-based jailbreaks is (1) STACKING multiple heterogeneous ciphers rather than one, and (2) a reinforcement-learning-based ADAPTIVE strategy (a gradient bandit) that dynamically selects cipher groups, lengths, and combinations from victim feedback so the attack resists adaptive/future defenses. The paper also empirically argues a counterintuitive claim: stronger reasoning helps LRMs shrug off SIMPLE jailbreaks but makes them MORE vulnerable to complex encrypted ones.
threat_model: Black-box adversary A with only API query access to a victim reasoning model M (no weights, gradients, or logits). A submits an encrypted adversarial prompt p* and observes M's textual output; M first produces a Chain-of-Thought (which acts as the decryption step) then a final answer. The attacker's goal is to make M emit hazardous content semantically matching an original unsafe prompt p while evading built-in and potentially adaptive safety mechanisms. Success is judged by an external LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o-mini) plus manual human review.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) SEAL's dynamic cipher flexibility makes it hard to defend against with existing or foreseeable countermeasures (framed as a strength but flagged as a limitation of the study's defensive value); (2) only the gradient bandit RL algorithm is explored -- alternatives like Epsilon-Greedy or Softmax-with-Value-Estimates are left to future work. Reviewer-noted caveats: the headline numbers are on a hand-curated 125-prompt subset selected specifically because it was consistently rejected by o4-mini (from 1,583 total), so absolute ASRs are not representative of the full benchmark; the curation was tuned to o4-mini's failures, which may bias cross-model comparisons. Success relies on a single automated judge (GPT-4o-mini), which the authors themselves show produces false positives (Table 7 gap, e.g., Sonnet 3.7 89.47% judge-only vs 85.6% after human filter), partially mitigated by manual review. Transferability, while best-in-class, drops sharply off the source model (34.29% on o4-mini, 58.10% on DeepSeek from Sonnet-3.7-crafted prompts). No defense/mitigation is proposed or evaluated.

datasets_used: Combined pool of 1,583 harmful requests, curated to a 125-prompt high-risk subset for main evaluation
quantitative_results: SEAL-adaptive ASR: 80.8% o4-mini, 84.8% o1-mini, 85.6% Claude 3.7 Sonnet, 84.0% Claude 3.5 Sonnet, 79.2% Gemini 2.0 Flash (M), 100% Gemini 2.0 Flash (H), 100% DeepSeek-R1
27.2-point ASR margin over best baseline (AutoDAN 53.6%) on o4-mini
Transferability from Sonnet-3.7-crafted prompts (Table 3): SEAL 34.29% on o4-mini and 58.10% on DeepSeek-R1, vs LACE 23.81%/42.86% and AutoDAN 15.63%/21.88%

Single-cipher ablation (Table 4): recovery >97% for all ciphers except Vigenere (44.85%, excluded); ASRs 27.48% Custom, 27.16% Caesar, 25.46% Atbash, 18.83% ASCII, 15.79% HEX, 15.16% Vigenere, 19.46% RW, 18.89% RL, 25.02% REW
Recovery rate declines with cipher length; Sonnet 3.7 recovers ~half of attacks by length 6
Of 1,583 prompts, 1,287 (81.30%) succeeded at least once and 469 (29.63%) jailbroke o4-mini with 3+ ciphers; 125-prompt subset drawn from the 296 consistently rejected
Prompt-structure ablation (Table 5, Sonnet 3.7): placing ciphertext in a separate <cipher> tag vs blended header boosts ASR by ~10-20% (e.g., length 2: 64.21% inside vs 53.6% original)
LLM-judge vs LLM+human (Table 7): e.g. o4-mini 86.57% -> 80.2%, Sonnet 3.7 89.47% -> 85.6%, Sonnet 3.5 89.97% -> 84.0%
